artifacts.content:
Hi Megan,

I’m finally sitting down with some coffee while the kids are buried in library books, so I wanted to actually write instead of just sending you half-coherent texts.

We’ve decided we’re completely done with MCAs for Ethan. You remember how bad last year was, but it really hit me when I looked back over everything in one place. In March 2024 during the MCA Reading test at Seward, he melted down so badly that the school social worker, Ms. Karen Lopez, had to pull him out of Room 214 and sit with him in the counseling office for about 90 minutes. He was crying, shaking, and telling her he “never wants to be alive on test days.” Hearing that secondhand from Karen made my stomach drop.

Then there was the sleep mess. From February through April 2024, I kept that little sleep log for his psychiatrist, Dr. Anil Desai at Fairview University of Minnesota Medical Center. Looking back, before every practice test and official date he was getting less than five hours of sleep for at least three nights in a row. Dr. Desai actually increased his sertraline from 25 mg to 50 mg on March 28, 2024, and he said outright that it was because the test-related anxiety wasn’t letting up.

And of course the April 16, 2025 panic episode during MCA season, when we ended up at the Hennepin Healthcare emergency department downtown, was the final straw. In our May 2, 2025 session at Riverside Child & Adolescent Clinic, Dr. Melissa Hart flat out told us he shouldn’t do any more high-stakes standardized tests for the foreseeable future.

Day to day he’s still our book-obsessed, Lego-spreading kid, and he’s thriving with projects and his little STEM club robots. I just want school to feel like that more often.

How are the twins holding up with winter? Send snow pictures if you get any decent light!

Love,
Laura

metadata.subject: Catching you up on Ethan and school

artifacts.content:
Hi Dr. Patel,

I hope your week is going smoothly. I wanted to check in about Ethan and get your perspective, since you’ve followed him since he was tiny and know his baseline better than anyone.

We’ve had a cluster of really intense reactions tied specifically to state testing over the last two years. During the March 2024 MCA Reading administration at Seward Montessori, he became so distressed that the school social worker, Ms. Karen Lopez, had to remove him from Room 214 and stay with him in the counseling office for nearly 90 minutes. She called me afterward to say he’d been crying inconsolably and repeating that he “never wants to be alive on test days,” which obviously scared all of us.

On top of that, his sleep tanked during the February–April 2024 testing window. I kept a detailed sleep log that we brought to his psychiatrist, Dr. Anil Desai, at Fairview University of Minnesota Medical Center. It showed that in the three nights leading up to each practice and official MCA date, he was sleeping less than five hours a night. Dr. Desai ended up increasing his sertraline from 25 mg to 50 mg on March 28, 2024, specifically because the test-related anxiety wasn’t budging.

More recently, on April 16, 2025, during last spring’s MCA period, Ethan had a severe panic episode at home in the evening—racing heart, chest tightness, hyperventilating—and we took him to the Hennepin Healthcare emergency department downtown. In our follow-up on May 2, 2025 at Riverside Child & Adolescent Clinic, his therapist, Dr. Melissa Hart, recommended that he avoid high-stakes standardized tests for the foreseeable future.

Day to day he’s doing much better with regular classroom work, projects, and his STEM club. I’d appreciate any thoughts you have on how we should be thinking about his physical health risks when this kind of stress ramps up again.

Warmly,
Laura Carlson

metadata.subject: Ethan’s anxiety episodes around testing – looking for your input

artifacts.content:
[Family Group Chat – “Carlsons + Jensens”]

Laura: Quick update on Ethan’s day – it was rough.

Laura: This morning at Seward they handed out another stack of MCA math practice packets. As soon as Ms. Ramirez started passing them around, Ethan went pale and ended up in the nurse’s office. He vomited twice and told them his chest felt tight and he was dizzy.

Pat (Mom): I’m glad they called. Leaving my Target shift early on East Lake Street wasn’t ideal, but of course I’ll always go get him.

Laura: Thank you again, Mom. The nurse said as soon as she mentioned it was “MCA practice,” he kind of folded in on himself. It was May 6, 2024 all over again – you leaving the Target on East Lake to pick him up after two rounds of throwing up in the Seward nurse’s office.

Bob: Poor kid. This is supposed to be school, not boot camp.

Dan: It’s not just one bad day either. Remember, between January 19 and March 8, 2025 they pulled him out of his favorite Friday science lab block and his small-group reading circle eight separate times for “MCA prep” because his winter FAST math score put him “on watch.” No wonder he says Fridays feel like punishment now.

Laura: Exactly. And that’s on top of last spring’s panic episode on April 16, 2025 that landed us at the Hennepin Healthcare emergency department downtown, plus everything Dr. Hart said on May 2 at Riverside Child & Adolescent Clinic about not putting him through any more high-stakes tests.

Pat: I hate that a piece of paper can do this to him. He’s so bright when he’s talking about those rockets with Grandpa.

Laura: Agreed. We’re going to focus on the projects and the joy stuff. Just wanted everyone in the loop.

Bob: Thanks for keeping us posted, kiddo.

Personal note – for my own brain, not to send anywhere.

I keep circling back to how much school has shifted for Ethan in the last two years. On paper, he’s a thriving 5th grader at Minneapolis Public Schools – Seward Montessori School, reading above grade level, happily building watersheds and coding little robots in STEM club. In reality, there’s this landmine called “testing season” that blows up everything we’ve built.

Timeline I don’t want to forget:

– March 2024: MCA Reading at Seward. Ethan is in Room 214. Within an hour, he’s in full panic. Ms. Karen Lopez, the school social worker, removes him from the room and sits with him in the counseling office for nearly 90 minutes while he cries and tells her he “never wants to be alive on test days.”

– Feb–April 2024: I track his sleep for Dr. Anil Desai at Fairview University of Minnesota Medical Center. Before every practice and official MCA date, he’s sleeping less than 5 hours a night for at least three nights straight. On March 28, 2024, Dr. Desai increases his sertraline from 25 mg to 50 mg, explicitly tying it to test-related anxiety.

– May 6, 2024: At Seward, they hand out MCA math practice packets. Ethan vomits twice in the nurse’s office, reports chest tightness and dizziness. Mom (Pat) leaves her part-time shift at the Target on East Lake Street to pick him up.

– April 16, 2025: Severe panic episode at home during MCA season; we go to the Hennepin Healthcare emergency department downtown. May 2, 2025: follow-up with Dr. Melissa Hart at Riverside Child & Adolescent Clinic, where she recommends no more high-stakes standardized tests for the foreseeable future.

Writing this down reminds me we’re not overreacting. This is a pattern, and opting him out is us choosing our kid over a data point.

Event: Ethan therapy – coping with school anxiety
Date: Wednesday, 2026-01-20
Time: 4:00–4:50 p.m.
Location: Riverside Child & Adolescent Clinic, Minneapolis
Attendees: Ethan, Laura (parent), Dr. Melissa Hart

Notes for myself so I don’t freeze up in the moment:

1. Check in with Dr. Hart about how Ethan is doing this winter overall – sleep, stomachaches before school, and how often he’s using his grounding techniques.

2. Bring up how he still tenses when he hears “MCA” or even “practice test.” Remind her of the big episodes:
   • March 2024 MCA Reading at Seward Montessori, when Ms. Karen Lopez had to pull him from Room 214 and sit with him in the counseling office for about 90 minutes while he cried and said he “never wants to be alive on test days.”
   • The February–April 2024 sleep log we showed Dr. Anil Desai at Fairview University of Minnesota Medical Center, showing less than 5 hours of sleep for at least three nights before each practice and official test date, and how that led to the sertraline increase from 25 mg to 50 mg on March 28, 2024.
   • April 16, 2025 panic episode that sent us to the Hennepin Healthcare emergency department downtown.

3. Ask for concrete ideas for what Ethan can do at school during testing windows now that we’re not having him take the MCAs at all. He loves his Friday science lab block and small-group reading circle, so I want alternatives that feel like learning, not punishment.

4. End with something positive: have Ethan share his excitement about the model rockets he’s planning to launch with Grandpa Bob this spring.

metadata.title: Calendar event – Ethan therapy with Dr. Hart

artifacts.content:
Hi Mr. Ellison and Ms. Nguyen,

I hope you’re both hanging in there as we hit the mid-winter slump. I wanted to follow up about Ethan’s schedule and how we handle things as we get closer to spring.

Looking back at last year, the way testing prep reshaped his Fridays was pretty hard on him. Between January 19 and March 8, 2025, he was pulled from his favorite Friday science lab block and his small-group reading circle eight separate times for “MCA prep” after his winter FAST math assessment flagged him as “on watch.” He still talks about how it felt like being punished for not being perfect on a test, especially since those are the two parts of the week he looks forward to most.

Layered on top of that were the more serious episodes you’re already aware of. In March 2024 during the MCA Reading test, the school social worker, Ms. Karen Lopez, had to remove him from Room 214 and stay with him in the counseling office for nearly 90 minutes because he was crying and saying he “never wants to be alive on test days.” Around that same window, from February–April 2024, his psychiatrist, Dr. Anil Desai at Fairview University of Minnesota Medical Center, reviewed our sleep log showing he was sleeping less than 5 hours a night in the days before each practice and official test. Dr. Desai increased his sertraline from 25 mg to 50 mg on March 28, 2024 specifically due to test-related anxiety.

Given all of this, we’re trying to be very intentional this year about not letting test prep displace the classes that help him feel competent and calm. I’m hoping we can brainstorm alternatives that keep him in science and reading as much as possible while still meeting any building expectations you’re working under.

Thank you for all you do for him.

Best,
Laura Carlson

Hi Dad,

Writing this while Ethan is sprawled on the living room rug with Max, explaining rocket fins to Olivia like he’s prepping for a NASA briefing. Moments like this make the school stuff feel even more surreal.

I wanted to explain a bit more why we’re so firm about keeping him out of the MCAs now. You know about some of it, but when I line it all up it’s pretty stark.

First, the physical side: on May 6, 2024, when his teacher started handing out MCA math practice packets at Seward, he ended up in the nurse’s office after vomiting twice and complaining of chest tightness and dizziness. Grandma Pat had to leave her part-time shift at the Target on East Lake Street early to pick him up. It’s hard not to see a direct line from “here’s another packet” to “kid is sick enough to go home.”

Then the emotional crashes: in March 2024 during the MCA Reading test in Room 214, the school social worker, Ms. Karen Lopez, had to pull him from the room and sit with him in the counseling office for nearly 90 minutes while he cried and said he “never wants to be alive on test days.” That phrase has been stuck in my head ever since.

And the medical piece: from February–April 2024, his sleep log showed less than 5 hours of sleep a night before each practice and official test. His psychiatrist, Dr. Anil Desai at Fairview University of Minnesota Medical Center, upped his sertraline from 25 mg to 50 mg on March 28, 2024, specifically because of test-related anxiety.

By the time we hit the severe panic episode on April 16, 2025 and landed at the Hennepin Healthcare emergency department downtown, and then heard Dr. Melissa Hart at Riverside Child & Adolescent Clinic on May 2, 2025 say he shouldn’t do any more high-stakes tests, it just confirmed what our guts already knew.

I know you sometimes worry we’re “going too easy” on him, but to me this is basic triage. We’ll keep pushing him on things that matter—kindness, perseverance, cleaning up his Lego explosions—but not on a test that keeps breaking him.

Love,
Laura

metadata.subject: Why we’re holding the line on testing for Ethan
